learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Intervals, Loops, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine checking your daily calendar. If two meetings overlap in time, you cannot attend both. Your goal is to verify whether your schedule has any conflicts.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine checking your daily calendar. If two meetings overlap in time, you cannot attend both. Your goal is to verify whether your schedule has any conflicts.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this pattern whenever you need to detect overlapping intervals after sorting them by their starting times.
learning.signals: Whenever you need to determine whether intervals overlap, think of sorting by start time followed by a linear scan.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an array of meeting time intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], determine whether a person could attend all meetings. Return true if no meetings overlap; otherwise return false.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare every meeting with every other meeting to detect overlaps.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: After sorting meetings by their start time, only adjacent meetings need to be checked. If a meeting starts before the previous one ends, an overlap exists.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: After sorting, overlapping intervals become adjacent.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever you need to determine whether intervals overlap, think of sorting by start time followed by a linear scan.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Sorting guarantees overlapping intervals become adjacent. Only one linear scan is needed after sorting.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever you need to determine whether intervals overlap, think of sorting by start time followed by a linear scan.
commonWrongPaths: Using <= instead of < when endpoints touch.
Sorting by end time instead of start time.

hints: Compare each interval with the previous one.
Return false immediately when overlap occurs.
commonMisconceptions: Using <= instead of < when endpoints touch.
Sorting by end time instead of start time.

pattern.name: Sorting + Interval Comparison
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever you need to determine whether intervals overlap, think of sorting by start time followed by a linear scan.

approaches.title: Compare Every Pair
approaches.idea: Compare every meeting with every other meeting to detect overlaps.

code.java: Nested loop solution omitted because of quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: Sorting + Linear Scan
approaches.idea: Sort intervals by start time and compare neighboring intervals for overlap.
algorithm: Compare current start with previous end.
Return false if overlap is found.

approaches.title: Sorting + Interval Comparison
approaches.idea: Sort all intervals by their starting time. Traverse the sorted intervals and compare each interval with the previous one. If the current meeting starts before the previous meeting ends, an overlap exists.
approaches.intuition: Sorting guarantees overlapping intervals become adjacent. Only one linear scan is needed after sorting.
algorithm: If current start < previous end, return false.

visualization.description: Sort meetings and compare neighboring intervals.
steps: Compare current start with previous end.

interviewFollowUps: How would you find the minimum number of meeting rooms?
Can this be solved without sorting?
What if meetings can touch at endpoints?
How would you merge overlapping intervals?
How would you return all conflicting meetings?
interviewTips: Explain why sorting simplifies overlap detection.
Show why only adjacent intervals matter.
State O(n log n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: After sorting, overlapping intervals become adjacent.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Sort → Compare Adjacent Intervals → Detect Overlap.

commonMistakes: Using <= instead of < when endpoints touch.
Sorting by end time instead of start time.
